verses.text: The sons of Levi; Gershon, Kohath, and Merari. And the sons of Kohath; Amram, Izhar, and Hebron, and Uzziel. And the children of Amram; Aaron, and Moses, and Miriam. The sons also of Aaron; Nadab, and Abihu, Eleazar, and Ithamar. Guidance: The Chronicler now comes to Levi — and this is no ordinary tribe, for Levi is the tribe of the priesthood and the temple, the very heart of the Chronicler's concern. From Levi's three sons (Gershon, Kohath, Merari) the genealogy moves swiftly to its center: through Kohath to Amram, and to Amram's children — 'Aaron, and Moses, and Miriam' — the three great figures of the Exodus, and then to the sons of Aaron, the founders of the priesthood. The chapter is showing us the LORD's provision of a mediating tribe for His people: Levi set apart for sacred service, and within Levi the house of Aaron set apart for the priesthood. The chapter is teaching us how central worship and mediation are in the life of God's people — the Chronicler gives the priestly tribe pride of place at the very center of his genealogies, framed between Judah (the kingly tribe, ch. 2-4) and the other tribes. The chapter is teaching us that the LORD did not leave His people without a way to draw near: He appointed Aaron and his sons to minister at the altar and make atonement. The naming of Nadab and Abihu (who would die for offering strange fire, Lev 10) alongside Eleazar and Ithamar reminds us, even at the outset, that priestly privilege carries priestly responsibility, and that the LORD is holy and must be approached as He commands. But the dominant note is the dignity of the calling: from Levi came Moses the lawgiver, Aaron the priest, and Miriam the prophetess — the tribe through whom the LORD gave His law and His worship to Israel. The center of the nation's genealogy is the tribe of those who serve the LORD's house.

Eleazar begat Phinehas, Phinehas begat Abishua, And Abishua begat Bukki, and Bukki begat Uzzi, And Uzzi begat Zerahiah, and Zerahiah begat Meraioth, Meraioth begat Amariah, and Amariah begat Ahitub, And Ahitub begat Zadok, and Zadok begat Ahimaaz, And Ahimaaz begat Azariah, and Azariah begat Johanan, And Johanan begat Azariah, (he it is that executed the priest's office in the temple that Solomon built in Jerusalem:) And Azariah begat Amariah, and Amariah begat Ahitub, And Ahitub begat Zadok, and Zadok begat Shallum, And Shallum begat Hilkiah, and Hilkiah begat Azariah, And Azariah begat Seraiah, and Seraiah begat Jehozadak, And Jehozadak went into captivity, when the LORD carried away Judah and Jerusalem by the hand of Nebuchadnezzar. Guidance: This is the high-priestly line — the unbroken succession of high priests from Eleazar (Aaron's son) and Phinehas (the zealous priest of Num 25, to whom the LORD gave a covenant of an everlasting priesthood) down through Zadok (David and Solomon's faithful high priest) to Seraiah and his son Jehozadak, 'who went into captivity, when the LORD carried away Judah and Jerusalem by the hand of Nebuchadnezzar.' Just as the royal line of David ran down to the exile (ch. 3), so the priestly line ran down to the same exile. The chapter is showing us the LORD's faithfulness in preserving an unbroken priesthood across the centuries, mediating between Him and His people generation after generation, until the catastrophe of Babylon. The chapter is teaching us that the priesthood, like the kingship, was a divine institution carried by the LORD through all the turns of Israel's history; the note that Azariah 'executed the priest's office in the temple that Solomon built' ties the line to the great house of worship that is the Chronicler's central concern. The chapter is teaching us, in Jehozadak's captivity, that even the sacred priesthood was not exempt from the judgment that fell on a sinful nation — the high priest himself went into exile when the LORD carried away Judah. And yet the line did not perish: Jehozadak's son was Joshua (Jeshua), who would return from Babylon to serve as high priest in the restored community (Hag 1:1; Zech 3) — so that this very genealogy assured the post-exilic readers that the priestly succession survived the exile and continued. The chapter is teaching us that the LORD preserved both throne and altar through the fire of judgment, keeping alive the offices of king and priest until the coming of Him who unites both — Jesus Christ, the King after the order of David and the Priest after the order of Melchizedek, in whom the long lines of David and of Aaron find their everlasting fulfillment.

Just as the royal line of David ran down to the exile (ch. 3), so the priestly line ran down to the same exile. The chapter is showing us the LORD's faithfulness in preserving an unbroken priesthood across the centuries, mediating between Him and His people generation after generation, until the catastrophe of Babylon. The chapter is teaching us that the priesthood, like the kingship, was a divine institution carried by the LORD through all the turns of Israel's history; the note that Azariah 'executed the priest's office in the temple that Solomon built' ties the line to the great house of worship that is the Chronicler's central concern. The chapter is teaching us, in Jehozadak's captivity, that even the sacred priesthood was not exempt from the judgment that fell on a sinful nation — the high priest himself went into exile when the LORD carried away Judah. And yet the line did not perish: Jehozadak's son was Joshua (Jeshua), who would return from Babylon to serve as high priest in the restored community (Hag 1:1; Zech 3) — so that this very genealogy assured the post-exilic readers that the priestly succession survived the exile and continued. The chapter is teaching us that the LORD preserved both throne and altar through the fire of judgment, keeping alive the offices of king and priest until the coming of Him who unites both — Jesus Christ, the King after the order of David and the Priest after the order of Melchizedek, in whom the long lines of David and of Aaron find their everlasting fulfillment.

The sons of Levi; Gershom, Kohath, and Merari. And these be the names of the sons of Gershom; Libni, and Shimei. And the sons of Kohath were, Amram, and Izhar, and Hebron, and Uzziel. The sons of Merari; Mahli, and Mushi. And these are the families of the Levites according to their fathers. Of Gershom; Libni his son, Jahath his son, Zimmah his son, Joah his son, Iddo his son, Zerah his son, Jeaterai his son. The sons of Kohath; Amminadab his son, Korah his son, Assir his son, Elkanah his son, and Ebiasaph his son, and Assir his son, Tahath his son, Uriel his son, Uzziah his son, and Shaul his son. And the sons of Elkanah; Amasai, and Ahimoth. As for Elkanah: the sons of Elkanah; Zophai his son, and Nahath his son, Eliab his son, Jeroham his son, Elkanah his son. And the sons of Samuel; the firstborn Vashni, and Abiah. The sons of Merari; Mahli, Libni his son, Shimei his son, Uzza his son, Shimea his son, Haggiah his son, Asaiah his son. Guidance: The Chronicler now traces the three great Levitical families — Gershom, Kohath, and Merari — the divisions among whom all the temple service was apportioned. And buried in the Kohathite line is a name of quiet significance: through Elkanah and Jeroham we come to 'the sons of Samuel' — Samuel the prophet, the kingmaker, the man who anointed both Saul and David. The chapter is revealing that Samuel was a Levite of the family of Kohath, which is why, though not a priest, he could minister before the LORD at the tabernacle from childhood (1 Sam 2-3). The chapter is showing us that the LORD raised up His servants from within the tribe set apart for His service; the genealogy quietly establishes Samuel's Levitical descent. The chapter is also showing us, in this same line, the name of Korah — the Kohathite who led the great rebellion against Moses and Aaron and perished (Num 16) — yet whose descendants did not perish but became honored temple servants and singers (the 'sons of Korah' of the Psalms). The chapter is teaching us that the LORD's grace can redeem a family marked by a father's terrible sin: from the line of Korah the rebel came generations of faithful worshipers. The chapter is teaching us that the children are not bound forever by the sins of the fathers; the LORD shows mercy to those who turn to Him, and the very name associated with rebellion became, in later generations, a name in the songbook of praise. From these three Levitical families — Gershom, Kohath, Merari — the LORD drew the whole company of those who served His house, prophets like Samuel and singers like the sons of Korah, a tribe wholly given to the ministry of God.

And these are they whom David set over the service of song in the house of the LORD, after that the ark had rest. And they ministered before the dwelling place of the tabernacle of the congregation with singing, until Solomon had built the house of the LORD in Jerusalem: and then they waited on their office according to their order. And these are they that waited with their children. Of the sons of the Kohathites: Heman a singer, the son of Joel, the son of Shemuel, the son of Elkanah, the son of Jeroham, the son of Eliel, the son of Toah, the son of Zuph, the son of Elkanah, the son of Mahath, the son of Amasai, the son of Elkanah, the son of Joel, the son of Azariah, the son of Zephaniah, the son of Tahath, the son of Assir, the son of Ebiasaph, the son of Korah, the son of Izhar, the son of Kohath, the son of Levi, the son of Israel. And his brother Asaph, who stood on his right hand, even Asaph the son of Berachiah, the son of Shimea, the son of Michael, the son of Baaseiah, the son of Malchiah, the son of Ethni, the son of Zerah, the son of Adaiah, the son of Ethan, the son of Zimmah, the son of Shimei, the son of Jahath, the son of Gershom, the son of Levi. And their brethren the sons of Merari stood on the left hand: Ethan the son of Kishi, the son of Abdi, the son of Malluch, the son of Hashabiah, the son of Amaziah, the son of Hilkiah, the son of Amzi, the son of Bani, the son of Shamer, the son of Mahli, the son of Mushi, the son of Merari, the son of Levi. Their brethren also the Levites were appointed unto all manner of service of the tabernacle of the house of God. Guidance: Now we reach a passage dear to the Chronicler's heart: the singers whom David set over 'the service of song in the house of the LORD, after that the ark had rest.' The three chief singers — Heman of the Kohathites, Asaph of the Gershomites (who stood on his right hand), and Ethan of the Merarites (on the left hand) — represent all three Levitical families, united in the ministry of praise. Their genealogies are traced all the way back to Levi and to Israel, grounding the temple music in the deepest roots of the covenant people. The chapter is showing us that the worship of God in song was not a casual or spontaneous thing but a divinely-ordered ministry, organized by David, staffed by Levites of every family, and continued 'according to their order' once Solomon built the temple. The chapter is teaching us how greatly the LORD values the ministry of praise: He appointed a whole company of skilled Levites to do nothing but lead His people in song before His dwelling place. The chapter is teaching us that sung worship is a sacred service — Heman, Asaph, and Ethan are remembered in Scripture not as entertainers but as ministers, and the psalms that bear their names (Ps 88 of Heman, the Asaph psalms, Ps 89 of Ethan) remain part of the church's praise to this day. The chapter is teaching us, in 'after that the ark had rest,' the right ordering of worship: once the LORD's presence had a settled place, His people gave themselves to continual praise. The chapter is teaching the post-exilic community, rebuilding the temple and restoring its worship, that the ministry of song was woven into the very fabric of the LORD's house by David himself — and so the returned Levites took up again the harps and the songs of their fathers. For us, this passage dignifies the work of all who lead God's people in praise: it is a calling traced back to David and ordained by God, and the God who set Heman, Asaph, and Ethan before His tabernacle is worthy of our every song.

But Aaron and his sons offered upon the altar of the burnt offering, and on the altar of incense, and were appointed for all the work of the place most holy, and to make an atonement for Israel, according to all that Moses the servant of God had commanded. And these are the sons of Aaron; Eleazar his son, Phinehas his son, Abishua his son, Bukki his son, Uzzi his son, Zerahiah his son, Meraioth his son, Amariah his son, Ahitub his son, Zadok his son, Ahimaaz his son. Guidance: The Chronicler draws a clear line between the work of the Levites (the service of song, the care of the tabernacle) and the unique work of Aaron and his sons: 'Aaron and his sons offered upon the altar of the burnt offering, and on the altar of incense... and to make an atonement for Israel, according to all that Moses the servant of God had commanded.' The priests alone approached the altar; the priests alone made atonement. The chapter is showing us the heart of the priestly ministry — atonement, the offering of sacrifice to cover the sins of the people and reconcile them to a holy God. The chapter is teaching us that sinful people cannot come to the holy LORD without a mediator and a sacrifice; the whole apparatus of altar, burnt offering, incense, and the most holy place existed to make atonement for Israel. The chapter is teaching us that this was done 'according to all that Moses... had commanded' — worship and atonement are not according to human invention but according to God's revealed command; the priests served by divine appointment and divine prescription. And the chapter is teaching us, supremely, to look beyond Aaron to the one whom Aaron foreshadowed. The endless succession of dying priests offering repeated sacrifices pointed to their own insufficiency and to the need of a better Priest and a better sacrifice. The whole Aaronic priesthood, making atonement year after year at the altar, was a long preparation for Jesus Christ, our great High Priest, who 'by one offering... hath perfected for ever them that are sanctified' (Heb 10:14), who entered not the earthly most holy place but heaven itself, and who made atonement not with the blood of bulls and goats but with His own blood, once for all. The chapter is teaching us that the LORD always intended a way of atonement for His people — and that every priest at the altar was a finger pointing forward to the cross.

But Aaron and his sons offered upon the altar of the burnt offering, and on the altar of incense, and were appointed for all the work of the place most holy, and to make an atonement for Israel, according to all that Moses the servant of God had commanded. And these are the sons of Aaron; Eleazar his son, Phinehas his son, Abishua his son, Bukki his son, Uzzi his son, Zerahiah his son, Meraioth his son, Amariah his son, Ahitub his son, Zadok his son, Ahimaaz his son. Guidance: The Chronicler draws a clear line between the work of the Levites (the service of song, the care of the tabernacle) and the unique work of Aaron and his sons: 'Aaron and his sons offered upon the altar of the burnt offering, and on the altar of incense... and to make an atonement for Israel, according to all that Moses the servant of God had commanded.' The priests alone approached the altar; the priests alone made atonement. The chapter is showing us the heart of the priestly ministry — atonement, the offering of sacrifice to cover the sins of the people and reconcile them to a holy God. The chapter is teaching us that sinful people cannot come to the holy LORD without a mediator and a sacrifice; the whole apparatus of altar, burnt offering, incense, and the most holy place existed to make atonement for Israel. The chapter is teaching us that this was done 'according to all that Moses... had commanded' — worship and atonement are not according to human invention but according to God's revealed command; the priests served by divine appointment and divine prescription. And the chapter is teaching us, supremely, to look beyond Aaron to the one whom Aaron foreshadowed. The endless succession of dying priests offering repeated sacrifices pointed to their own insufficiency and to the need of a better Priest and a better sacrifice. The whole Aaronic priesthood, making atonement year after year at the altar, was a long preparation for Jesus Christ, our great High Priest, who 'by one offering... hath perfected for ever them that are sanctified' (Heb 10:14), who entered not the earthly most holy place but heaven itself, and who made atonement not with the blood of bulls and goats but with His own blood, once for all. Now these are their dwelling places throughout their castles in their coasts, of the sons of Aaron, of the families of the Kohathites: for theirs was the lot. And they gave them Hebron in the land of Judah, and the suburbs thereof round about it. But the fields of the city, and the villages thereof, they gave to Caleb the son of Jephunneh. And to the sons of Aaron they gave the cities of Judah, namely, Hebron, the city of refuge, and Libnah with her suburbs, and Jattir, and Eshtemoa, with their suburbs, And Hilen with her suburbs, Debir with her suburbs, And Ashan with her suburbs, and Bethshemesh with her suburbs: And out of the tribe of Benjamin; Geba with her suburbs, and Alemeth with her suburbs, and Anathoth with her suburbs. All their cities throughout their families were thirteen cities. And unto the sons of Kohath, which were left of the family of that tribe, were cities given out of the half tribe, namely, out of the half tribe of Manasseh, by lot, ten cities. And to the sons of Gershom throughout their families out of the tribe of Issachar, and out of the tribe of Asher, and out of the tribe of Naphtali, and out of the tribe of Manasseh in Bashan, thirteen cities. Unto the sons of Merari were given by lot, throughout their families, out of the tribe of Reuben, and out of the tribe of Gad, and out of the tribe of Zebulun, twelve cities. And the children of Israel gave to the Levites these cities with their suburbs. And they gave by lot out of the tribe of the children of Judah, and out of the tribe of the children of Simeon, and out of the tribe of the children of Benjamin, these cities, which are called by their names. And the residue of the families of the sons of Kohath had cities of their coasts out of the tribe of Ephraim. And they gave unto them, of the cities of refuge, Shechem in mount Ephraim with her suburbs; they gave also Gezer with her suburbs, And Jokmeam with her suburbs, and Bethhoron with her suburbs, And Aijalon with her suburbs, and Gathrimmon with her suburbs: And out of the half tribe of Manasseh; Aner with her suburbs, and Bileam with her suburbs, for the family of the remnant of the sons of Kohath. Unto the sons of Gershom were given out of the family of the half tribe of Manasseh, Golan in Bashan with her suburbs, and Ashtaroth with her suburbs: And out of the tribe of Issachar; Kedesh with her suburbs, Daberath with her suburbs, And Ramoth with her suburbs, and Anem with her suburbs: And out of the tribe of Asher; Mashal with her suburbs, and Abdon with her suburbs, And Hukok with her suburbs, and Rehob with her suburbs: And out of the tribe of Naphtali; Kedesh in Galilee with her suburbs, and Hammon with her suburbs, and Kirjathaim with her suburbs. Unto the rest of the children of Merari were given out of the tribe of Zebulun, Rimmon with her suburbs, Tabor with her suburbs: And on the other side Jordan by Jericho, on the east side of Jordan, were given them out of the tribe of Reuben, Bezer in the wilderness with her suburbs, and Jahzah with her suburbs, Kedemoth also with her suburbs, and Mephaath with her suburbs: And out of the tribe of Gad; Ramoth in Gilead with her suburbs, and Mahanaim with her suburbs, And Heshbon with her suburbs, and Jazer with her suburbs. Guidance: The chapter closes with the dwelling places of the Levites — the cities given to them, by lot, scattered throughout the territories of all the other tribes. This is the outworking of a profound arrangement: Levi received no single block of land as an inheritance, no tribal territory of his own, but instead was given forty-eight cities (with their pasture-lands) spread across the whole nation, including the cities of refuge like Hebron, Shechem, and Bezer. The chapter is showing us the LORD's wise design: the priestly and worshiping tribe was deliberately dispersed among all the others, so that the knowledge of God, the ministry of the law, and the presence of those who served the LORD's house would be within reach of every tribe and region. The chapter is teaching us why Levi had no land of his own — because, as Moses had said, 'the LORD is their inheritance' (Deut 18:2; Josh 13:33). The Levites were to live not by territorial possession but by the LORD Himself and by the offerings of His people; their portion was God. The chapter is teaching us a pattern for all who serve the LORD: their treasure and security are not in earthly holdings but in God Himself. The chapter is teaching us, too, that worship and the teaching of God's word were meant to permeate the entire land — no tribe was left without Levites in its midst, no region without the cities of those set apart for God. And the inclusion of the cities of refuge among the Levitical cities is rich with mercy: the very towns where the man who killed unintentionally could flee for safety were entrusted to the priestly tribe, so that justice and mercy met in the places of God's servants — a beautiful foreshadowing of the refuge that sinners find in Christ, our great High Priest, to whom we flee and in whom we are safe. The genealogy of Levi thus ends not in a list of conquests or kings but in a picture of a tribe given wholly to God, scattered like salt through the land, possessing no inheritance but the LORD, and holding within their cities the refuge of the guilty. So the Chronicler sets the worship and ministry of God at the very heart and throughout the whole of the life of Israel.
